At Google, our goal has always been to improve the lives of as many people as possible through technology. Every time people use technology to solve a real problem — from finding an answer for any question, to screening for disease from the sound of a cough — we take another step towards what we set out to achieve. AI is making it possible to solve problems for people, economies, science and society faster than ever, but those solutions are only possible if everyone, everywhere, has access to these tools.
As leaders gather this week for the AI Impact Summit in India, we're announcing new partnerships and programs to accelerate progress globally, by building new infrastructure, expanding connectivity and access, empowering governments, advancing science, helping people learn new skills and building useful and safe products for everyone.

## Investing in infrastructure, science and government capacity
This work starts with a strong foundation. We recently announced a [$15 billion investment](https://blog.google/intl/en-in/company-news/our-first-ai-hub-in-india-powered-by-a-15-billion-investment/) to establish foundational AI infrastructure in India, and have been building subsea cables in the [Pacific,](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/infrastructure/honomoana-and-tabua-subsea-cables-connect-south-pacific) [Africa](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/infrastructure/investing-in-connectivity-and-growth-for-africa?e=48754805) and [around the world](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/infrastructure/talaylink-subsea-cable-to-connect-australia-and-thailand). Today we are announcing the [America-India Connect initiative](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/infrastructure/america-india-connect-infrastructure-connects-four-continents), which will deliver new strategic fiber-optic routes to increase the reach, reliability and resilience of digital connectivity between the U.S., India and multiple locations across the Southern Hemisphere.
To take advantage of the opportunities this infrastructure creates, we must also invest in people and the organizations working to solve critical challenges. Governments and public sector organizations are in a unique position to shape AI’s trajectory. While [new data](https://indexaiglobalpublicservices.publicfirst.co/) shows 74% of public servants globally are already using AI, only 18% believe their governments are using it effectively. To help bridge this gap, we're announcing several new initiatives:

- A $30 million Google.org Global [AI for Government Innovation Impact Challenge](https://blog.google/company-news/outreach-and-initiatives/google-org/global-impact-challenge-ai-government-2026/) to support partnerships that transform public services using AI — from everyday essential services to complex societal challenges.
- A $30 million Google.org [AI for Science Impact Challenge](https://blog.google/company-news/outreach-and-initiatives/google-org/impact-challenge-ai-science-open-call/) to support researchers globally who are using AI to drive scientific breakthroughs.
- Google DeepMind establishes [a new partnership with Indian government bodies and local institutions](http://deepmind.google/blog/accelerating-discovery-in-india-through-ai-powered-science-and-education/) to unlock new discoveries in science and education. This includes providing access to its frontier AI for Science models and powering innovation hubs with GenAI assistants, among other projects. This forms part of Google DeepMind’s broader [National Partnerships for AI](https://deepmind.google/national-partnerships-for-ai/) initiative — working with governments to broaden access to frontier AI capabilities for national priorities.
- The [Google Center for Climate Technology](https://blog.google/intl/en-in/company-news/outreach-initiatives/announcing-our-new-climate-tech-collaboration-in-india/) in collaboration with the office of the Principal Scientific Advisor to the Government of India, to accelerate research and adoption of scalable AI-powered climate solutions.

## Creating opportunity and capacity to solve problems with AI skills
Equipping people and civil servants with AI skills empowers them to use AI tools to solve problems at the

Claude Sonnet 4.6 is our most capable Sonnet model yet. It’s a full upgrade of the model’s skills across coding, computer use, long-context reasoning, agent planning, knowledge work, and design. Sonnet 4.6 also features a 1M token context window in beta.
For those on our [Free and Pro plans](https://claude.com/pricing), Claude Sonnet 4.6 is now the default model in [claude.ai](https://claude.ai/redirect/website.v1.b0d4bb1c-1687-42ad-aca4-c04e6bd6c331) and [Claude Cowork](https://claude.com/product/cowork). [Pricing](https://claude.com/pricing#api) remains the same as Sonnet 4.5, starting at $3/$15 per million tokens.
Sonnet 4.6 brings much-improved coding skills to more of our users. Improvements in consistency, instruction following, and more have made developers with early access prefer Sonnet 4.6 to its predecessor by a wide margin. They often even prefer it to our smartest model from November 2025, Claude Opus 4.5.
Performance that would have previously required reaching for an Opus-class model—including on real-world, economically valuable [office tasks](https://artificialanalysis.ai/evaluations/gdpval-aa)—is now available with Sonnet 4.6. The model also shows a major improvement in computer use skills compared to prior Sonnet models.
As with every new Claude model, we’ve run [extensive safety evaluations](https://anthropic.com/claude-sonnet-4-6-system-card) of Sonnet 4.6, which overall showed it to be as safe as, or safer than, our other recent Claude models. Our safety researchers concluded that Sonnet 4.6 has “a broadly warm, honest, prosocial, and at times funny character, very strong safety behaviors, and no signs of major concerns around high-stakes forms of misalignment.”

## Computer use
Almost every organization has software it can’t easily automate: specialized systems and tools built before modern interfaces like APIs existed. To have AI use such software, users would previously have had to build bespoke connectors. But a model that can use a computer the way a person does changes that equation.
In October 2024, we were the [first to introduce](https://www.anthropic.com/news/3-5-models-and-computer-use) a general-purpose computer-using model. At the time, we wrote that it was “still experimental—at times cumbersome and error-prone,” but we expected rapid improvement. [OSWorld](https://os-world.github.io/), the standard benchmark for AI computer use, shows how far our models have come. It presents hundreds of tasks across real software (Chrome, LibreOffice, VS Code, and more) running on a simulated computer. There are no special APIs or purpose-built connectors; the model sees the computer and interacts with it in much the same way a person would: clicking a (virtual) mouse and typing on a (virtual) keyboard.
Across sixteen months, our Sonnet models have made steady gains on OSWorld. The improvements can also be seen beyond benchmarks: early Sonnet 4.6 users are seeing human-level capability in tasks like navigating a complex spreadsheet or filling out a multi-step web form, before pulling it all together across multiple browser tabs.
The model certainly still lags behind the most skilled humans at using computers. But the rate of progress is remarkable nonetheless. It means that computer use is much more useful for a range of work tasks—and that substantially more capable models are within reach.
Scores prior to Claude Sonnet 4.5 were measured on the original OSWorld; scores from Sonnet 4.5 onward use OSWorld-Verified. OSWorld-Verified (released July 2025) is an in-place upgrade of the original OSWorld benchmark, with updates to task quality, evaluation grading, and infrastructure.
At the same time, computer use poses risks: malicious actors can attempt to hijack the model by hiding instructions on websites in what’s known as a prompt injection attack. We’ve been working to improve our models’ resistance to prompt injections—our [safety evaluations](https://anthropic.com/claude-sonnet-4-6-system-card) show that Sonnet 4.6 is a major improvement compared to its predecessor, Sonnet 4.5, and performs similarly to Opus 4.6. You can find out more about how to mitigate prompt injections and other safety concerns in [our API docs](https://pla

}

# 📊 AI Agent Industry Adoption 2026 — Research Report

## Executive Summary
AI agents are transitioning from experimental to operational across enterprise sectors in 2026, with significant infrastructure investment, rising government adoption, and maturing healthcare applications.

---

## 1. Enterprise Use Cases

### Key Findings
| Area | Status | Source |
|------|--------|--------|
| Customer support automation | Active deployment for ticket resolution | Forbes, Feb 2026 |
| Report compilation | Mainstream adoption | Forbes, Feb 2026 |
| Inventory management | Production deployment | Forbes, Feb 2026 |
| Multi-step web forms | Human-level capability achieved | Anthropic, Feb 2026 |
| Complex spreadsheets | Agentic navigation operational | Anthropic, Feb 2026 |

### Agentic Workflow Architecture
Modern AI agents follow an **Input → Task → Output** pattern:
- **Input**: Trigger event (email, alert, scheduled job)
- **Task**: AI-driven decision-making without rigid step definition
- **Output**: Deliverable result to correct destination

**Source:** Bernard Marr, "The Beginner's Blueprint For Building AI Agents That Handle Your Toughest Business Tasks," *Forbes*, Feb 25, 2026  
`https://www.forbes.com/sites/bernardmarr/2026/02/25/the-beginners-blueprint-for-building-ai-agents-that-handle-your-toughest-business-tasks/`

---

## 2. Industry Statistics

### Public Sector Adoption
| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Public servants using AI | **74%** | Google AI Impact Summit, Mar 2026 |
| Believe government uses AI effectively | **18%** | Google AI Impact Summit, Mar 2026 |
| Gap | **56 percentage points** | — |

### Infrastructure Investment
- **$15 billion**: Google's India AI infrastructure commitment (2026)
- **$30 million**: Google.org AI for Government Innovation Impact Challenge
- **$30 million**: Google.org AI for Science Impact Challenge

**Source:** "AI Impact Summit 2026: How We're Partnering to Make AI Work for Everyone," *Google Blog*, Mar 2026  
`https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/technology/ai/ai-impact-summit-2026-india/`

### Model Performance Trends
- **Claude Sonnet 4.6** (Anthropic, Feb 17, 2026): Frontier performance now available at mid-tier pricing ($3/$15 per million tokens)
- Coding improvements: Developers prefer Sonnet 4.6 over prior Opus-class models for real-world office tasks
- Computer use (OSWorld benchmark): Steady 16-month improvement trajectory; human-level capability achieved for navigation tasks

**Source:** "Introducing Claude Sonnet 4.6," *Anthropic*, Feb 17, 2026  
`https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-sonnet-4-6`

---

## 3. Sector Applications

### Healthcare
| Application | Details | Source |
|-------------|---------|--------|
| Radiology | **Merlin 3D vision-language model**: Stronger off-the-shelf performance across 3 distinct hospital sites vs. training center | *Nature*, Mar 4, 2026 |
| Abdominal CT analysis | Trained on CT scans + radiology reports + EHR data | *Nature*, Mar 4, 2026 |
| Kidney disease research | AI-driven protein structure modeling advances | *Nature*, Feb 18, 2026 |

**Source:** "Radiology AI makes consistent diagnoses using 3D images from different health centres," *Nature*, Mar 4, 2026  
`https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00645-2`

### Scientific/Research

- Google DeepMind: National Partnerships for AI initiative — government partnerships for frontier AI access

**Source:** "Claude on Mars," *Anthropic*, Jan 30, 2026  
`https://www.anthropic.com/mars`

### Cross-Sector Trends
- **Low-code/no-code platforms**: Democratizing agent creation (non-experts building production agents via natural language)
- **Agent construction framework**: Inputs/Tasks/Outputs methodology becoming standard

---

## 4. Limitations & Research Gaps
- **Financial services**: No direct 2026 sector-specific case studies captured in this search
- **Manufacturing**: General automation trends noted but specific 2026 AI agent deployments require additional sector search
- **Market sizing**: General investment figures available; granular market size projections not captured due to access restrictions on major analyst sites (Gartner, McKinsey)

---

| Google AI Impact Summit | Mar 2026 | Public sector adoption statistics |
| *Nature* | Mar 4, 2026 | Healthcare radiology validation |
| Anthropic | Feb 17, 2026 | Computer use capability benchmarks |
| Anthropic | Jan 30, 2026 | Space exploration agent deployment |

---

arguments.content:
# SaaS Sales Deck Outline
## 15-Slide B2B Presentation Structure

**Flow:** Hook → Problem → Solution → Product → Social Proof → Pricing → CTA

---

## Section 1: The Hook

### Slide 1: Title Slide
- **Key Points:**
  - Your company name & tagline
  - Date
  - Presenter name + company
  - Clean, high-impact hero imagery
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Full-bleed background image or gradient
  - Centered logo
  - Minimal text, maximum whitespace
  - Brand colors prominent

---

### Slide 2: Opening Statement / Big Idea
- **Key Points:**
  - One sentence that captures the transformation you enable
  - Example: "The average enterprise loses 40% of potential revenue to inefficiency. We're here to change that."
  - Sets the emotional stakes
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Large typography (3-5 words max)
  - Striking icon or simple illustration
  - Dark mode with accent color text

---

### Slide 3: The Landscape
- **Key Points:**
  - Current market context
  - What's changing in the industry
  - Why the status quo is unsustainable
  - Hook the audience into the narrative
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Market trend chart or timeline
  - 3-4 key statistics
  - Icons representing industry pain points

---

## Section 2: The Problem

### Slide 4: Pain Point Deep Dive
- **Key Points:**
  - Identify the core problem you solve
  - Make it relatable and specific
  - Use customer language/terminology
  - Quantify the pain (time lost, money wasted)
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Before/after comparison or split screen
  - Pain point icons (red/orange accent)
  - Bullet points with icons

---

### Slide 5: The Cost of Inaction
- **Key Points:**
  - What happens if they don't solve this problem?
  - Competitors who are solving it now
  - Risk of falling behind
  - Industry benchmarks showing the gap
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Urgency-inducing imagery
  - Chart showing widening gap/cost over time
  - Calendar/timeline visualization

---

## Section 3: The Solution

### Slide 6: Solution Overview
- **Key Points:**
  - Introduce your approach (not product yet)
  - Philosophy/methodology
  - "Imagine if you could..." framing
  - The 3-4 key benefits of your approach
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Clean diagram or framework visual
  - Center concept with supporting elements
  - White/light background with brand colors

---

### Slide 7: How It Works (The Mechanism)
- **Key Points:**
  - Simple 3-step process
  - What inputs/transformations/outputs occur
  - Keep technical details minimal
  - Focus on the "aha" moment
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Flow diagram or process visualization
  - 3-column or 3-step horizontal layout
  - Icons representing each phase
  - Arrow connectors

---

## Section 4: The Product

### Slide 8: Product Demo / Screenshots
- **Key Points:**
  - Show, don't tell (key screens/features)
  - Highlight 3-5 core capabilities
  - Annotated screenshots
  - Focus on ease of use and value
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Product screenshots/mockups
  - Device frames (laptop/phone/tablet)
  - Callouts highlighting key features
  - Clean background, no clutter

---

### Slide 9: The "Aha" Feature
- **Key Points:**
  - The one feature that differentiates you
  - Why it matters specifically to this audience
  - How it solves the pain point from Slide 4
  - ROI / time savings quantified
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Feature spotlight with large screenshot
  - Before/after split or comparison
  - Impact metric highlighted (time saved, cost reduced)
  - Animated GIF or video thumbnail if presenting live

---

### Slide 10: Integration & Compatibility
- **Key Points:**
  - Works with their existing stack
  - Key integrations (CRMs, ERPs, etc.)
  - API availability
  - Security/compliance certifications
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Integration ecosystem diagram
  - Logo grid of compatible tools
  - Trust badges/security certifications
  - Integration timeline ("Set up in 5 minutes")

---

## Section 5: Social Proof

### Slide 11: Customer Success Story
- **Key Points:**
  - Specific customer name (with permission)
  - Industry-relevant example
  - Challenge → Solution → Results narrative
  - Concrete metrics (% improvement, time saved, ROI)
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Customer logo with quote
  - Results in large numbers/statistics
  - Before/after metrics
  - Headshot of customer contact (optional)

---

### Slide 12: Credibility & Logos
- **Key Points:**
  - Clients/customers (logo bar)
  - Awards & recognition
  - Analyst mentions (Gartner, Forrester, etc.)
  - Funding/traction if relevant
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Logo cloud or grid
  - "As featured in" media logos
  - Award badges
  - Trust indicators (SOC 2, GDPR, etc.)

---

## Section 6: Pricing

### Slide 13: Pricing Tiers
- **Key Points:**
  - 3 clear tiers (Standard, Professional, Enterprise)
  - What's included in each
  - ROI calculator or typical savings
  - "Most popular" tier highlighted
  - Price anchoring strategy (if applicable)
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - 3-column pricing table
  - Feature checkmarks/comparison
  - CTA button on each tier
  - "Contact us" for Enterprise

---

### Slide 14: Implementation Timeline
- **Key Points:**
  - Quick time-to-value
  - Week-by-week or month-by-month milestones
  - Support included
  - Success metrics they'll see
  - Minimize implementation risk
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Timeline graphic
  - 30-60-90 day milestones
  - Icons for each phase
  - "Value realization" markers

---

## Section 7: Call to Action

### Slide 15: Next Steps / Q&A
- **Key Points:**
  - Clear next step ("Schedule a pilot," "Start free trial")
  - Contact information
  - QR code to schedule/demo
  - Open for questions
  - Thank you + contact info
- **Visuals/Layout:**
  - Big, bold CTA text
  - QR code or calendar booking link
  - Contact details (email, phone)
  - Simple, clean design
  - Logo and tagline

---

## Design Guidelines

### Overall Style
- **Colors:** Primary brand color + secondary accent + neutrals
- **Typography:** Sans-serif, accessible contrast
- **White space:** Generous use for breathing room
- **Consistency:** Same header/footer throughout

### Typography
- Headlines: 40-60pt
- Subheadlines: 24-32pt
- Body: 16-20pt
- Minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio

### Visual Assets Needed
- Hero images for Sections 1, 2, 5
- Product screenshots (10-15 total)
- Customer logos (8-10)
- Integration partner logos (6-8)
- Icons for key concepts (20-25)

---

## Speaker Notes Template

For each slide, prepare:
1. **Transition:** How you move from previous slide
2. **Hook:** Opening statement to capture attention
3. **Key message:** One sentence takeaway
4. **Story/Example:** Concrete illustration
5. **Bridge:** Next slide connection

---

*Document created: March 18, 2026*
*Purpose: B2B SaaS Sales Presentation Structure*
*Target: Enterprise decision-makers, 15-slide format*
